sound_type: bark + screaming vixen call -- two-part vocalization combining the short, sharp territorial bark and the blood-curdling vixen scream (breeding season), the 72nd acoustic element, the most dramatic acoustic contrast in any single species, two vocalization modes in one entry
ecosystem: open country -- grasslands, meadows, agricultural fields, pastures, clearcuts, prairie remnants, rural roadsides, and open woodland margins. The FIRST truly open-country species in the mammalian era. Not forest, not creek, not edge. OPEN.
season: year-round resident throughout Washington State, from sea level to alpine meadows

coordinates.description: Reference for open-country habitat in the Puget Sound lowlands. Red Foxes inhabit grasslands, agricultural margins, meadows, and open areas throughout Washington. Bark and screaming vixen call most commonly heard at dawn and dusk in open country during the winter breeding season (December-February), with barks audible year-round at territory boundaries.

taxonomy_note.description: Twelfth mammalian entry. Tenth mammalian species. Tenth Carnivora, first Canidae. NEW FAMILY (31st). The FIRST non-musteloid carnivore. Canidae breaks the nine-degree musteloid monopoly in Carnivora. The fox enters the engine.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Vulpes from Latin vulpes, meaning 'fox' -- the tautonymic binomial. Vulpes vulpes = 'fox fox.' The name is self-referential because the Red Fox is the archetypal fox -- the species that defines the word. Common name 'fox' derives from Proto-Germanic *fuhsaz, related to Proto-Indo-European *puḱ- meaning 'tail' or 'bushy-tailed one.' The fox is named for its tail.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Vulpes -- twelve species worldwide. The true foxes. V. vulpes (Red Fox) is the most widespread wild carnivore on Earth -- native range spans North America, Europe, Asia, and North Africa. Introduced to Australia.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Carnivora -- tenth entry. FOURTH family represented (Mustelidae 6, Mephitidae 1, Procyonidae 1, Canidae 1). The FIRST non-musteloid carnivore. Canidae is in the suborder Caniformia but NOT in the superfamily Musteloidea. This breaks the nine-entry musteloid streak. Third consecutive omnivore in the order.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- twelfth mammalian entry. Tenth mammalian species. Mammalian representation rises to 16.7% (12 of 72 entries). The mammalian era (degrees 61-71) is now eleven degrees deep. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), six families (Castoridae, Mustelidae, Mephitidae, Procyonidae, Canidae).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Canidae's FIRST entry at degree 71. The 31st family in the engine. NEW FAMILY. Canidae is the dog family within the suborder Caniformia, containing 13 genera and approximately 37 species (wolves, coyotes, foxes, jackals, wild dogs). Canidae is NOT musteloid -- it belongs to the superfamily Cynoidea (or is placed outside Musteloidea), breaking the nine-consecutive-musteloid-carnivore streak.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Bark: approximately 500-2,000 Hz, short sharp burst. Vixen scream: approximately 800-3,500 Hz, rising then falling, sustained. Wide range reflecting the two dramatically different vocalization types.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Bark: 800-1,500 Hz (the sharp territorial burst band). Vixen scream: 1,200-2,500 Hz (the sustained eerie wail band).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. TWO DISTINCT VOCALIZATIONS. (1) Bark -- short, sharp, explosive territorial call. One to several per sequence, spaced 3-8 seconds apart. Heard year-round but peaks in breeding season. (2) Vixen scream -- a sustained, blood-curdling shriek lasting 1-3 seconds, often repeated. Produced primarily by females during the breeding season (December-February). Frequently mistaken for a human scream. The most dramatic acoustic contrast in any single species -- short bark vs. sustained scream.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Bark: 0.1-0.3 seconds per bark, often in series of 3-8 barks with 3-8 second intervals. Vixen scream: 1-3 seconds per scream, often repeated 3-10 times in sequence with brief pauses. Barking sessions can last several minutes. Screaming bouts can persist for 10-30 minutes during peak breeding.
song_metrics.note_structure: Bark: single explosive note, percussive, no harmonic complexity. Sharp onset, rapid decay. Vixen scream: complex, multiharmonic, with a rising-then-falling pitch contour. The scream starts abruptly, sustains at peak intensity, then drops. The contrast between the two is the species' acoustic signature.
song_metrics.note_types: Bark (short, sharp, explosive, territorial), vixen scream (sustained, eerie, blood-curdling, breeding), gekkering (rapid staccato chattering during close-range encounters), whine (submissive/juvenile contact call), growl (close-range aggression). The bark/scream duality is the Red Fox's signature -- territorial clarity meets reproductive drama.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Bark: repetitive series -- bark...bark...bark -- with regular intervals. No complex phrase structure. The bark is a beacon, not a song. Vixen scream: repeated screams with irregular intervals, sometimes overlapping between multiple foxes. The scream is a broadcast, not a conversation.
song_metrics.timbre: Bark: sharp, dry, clipped -- often described as 'yapping' or 'yelping.' High-pitched for a carnivore. Metallic quality. Vixen scream: extraordinary -- a raspy, shrieking wail that is simultaneously mammalian and alien. The scream has been compared to a woman screaming, a cat being killed, or a banshee. No other PNW mammal produces a sound this disturbing.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. The vixen scream carries 500+ meters in open country. The bark carries 300-500 meters. Both are LOUD vocalizations -- intentionally carrying across territory. The scream is one of the loudest mammalian vocalizations in the PNW. E=5 reflects the combined impact: a bark that punctuates the night and a scream that stops you in your tracks.
song_metrics.tempo: Bark: regular, metronomic, 3-8 second intervals. The bark is patient. Vixen scream: irregular, unpredictable timing. The scream erupts without warning and stops without resolution. The tempo contrast mirrors the behavioral contrast: territorial order vs. reproductive urgency.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL AND REPRODUCTIVE COMMUNICATION. Bark: year-round territorial advertisement and contact call between mated pairs. Most frequent at dawn, dusk, and night. Vixen scream: primarily produced by females during the winter breeding season (December-February in Washington). The scream is a mate-attraction and estrus-advertisement call. Both vocalizations are crepuscular/nocturnal -- heard at dawn, dusk, and through the night.
song_metrics.regional_variation: NO documented regional variation in bark structure. Vixen screams show individual variation in pitch and duration but no geographic dialects. The Red Fox's vocalizations are remarkably consistent across the species' enormous range.
song_metrics.key_property: BARK + SCREAMING VIXEN CALL -- the 72nd acoustic element. Two-part vocalization: bark (short, sharp, territorial) and vixen scream (sustained, blood-curdling, breeding season). E=5. The MOST DRAMATIC ACOUSTIC CONTRAST in any single species. The bark is order; the scream is chaos. The scream is one of the most dramatic sounds in the PNW night -- frequently mistaken for a human scream.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized canid. 900-1,120 mm total length (including tail), 3.6-6.8 kg. Lean, agile build -- unlike the stocky raccoon (70) or the compact mustelids (63-68). Long legs relative to body size. Narrow muzzle. Erect, pointed ears. The body plan is built for speed, stealth, and endurance in open country.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 71, the diagnostic features are: (1) ORANGE-RED FUR -- the defining color. Rich reddish-orange over the back, sides, and head. The most chromatically warm-toned mammal in the engine. (2) BLACK STOCKINGS -- lower legs and feet are black. (3) WHITE-TIPPED TAIL -- the bushy tail (the 'brush') always has a white tip, diagnostic at any distance. (4) BLACK EAR BACKS -- the backs of the large, pointed ears are black. (5) WHITE CHEST AND BELLY -- pale undersides contrast with the red-orange dorsum.
physical_description.fur_detail: BASE: Rich reddish-orange on the back, sides, head, and upper tail. The red varies from deep russet to bright orange depending on individual and season. Winter coat is thicker and more vivid. MARKINGS: Black lower legs ('stockings'), black ear backs, white chest/belly, white-tipped tail. The white tail tip is ALWAYS present and is the field mark -- even at distance, even at dusk, the white tail tip identifies the fox. VARIANTS: Cross fox (red-orange with dark cross pattern on shoulders) and silver fox (melanistic) are color morphs of the same species, not separate taxa.
physical_description.dental_detail: 42 teeth. Canidae dental formula. Long, slender canines for piercing. Well-developed carnassials for shearing. Less specialized than mustelid dentition -- the canid jaw is versatile, suited for the fox's omnivorous diet (rodents, rabbits, birds, insects, fruit, garbage).
physical_description.field_id: At open country, meadow edges, or rural roadsides: a medium-sized, lean canid with vivid reddish-orange fur, black stockings, black ear backs, and a bushy tail with a WHITE TIP. Moves with a light, fluid trot. The gait is distinctive -- the fox trots in a nearly straight line, with hindfoot tracks placed directly on forefoot tracks (direct-registering). The white tail tip bounces and flashes as the fox moves. Distinguished from Coyote by smaller size, more slender build, orange color (not gray-brown), and the white tail tip (coyotes have black tail tips).

nodes.title: Canidae Arrives -- 31st Family, First Non-Musteloid Carnivore
nodes.description: The 31st family in the engine. NEW FAMILY. The FIRST non-musteloid carnivore. Canidae breaks the nine-entry, nine-degree musteloid monopoly in Carnivora. The dog family enters. The fox is the ambassador.

nodes.title: Bark + Screaming Vixen Call -- 72nd Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Two-part vocalization: bark (short, sharp, territorial) and vixen scream (sustained, blood-curdling, breeding season). E=5. The most dramatic acoustic contrast in any single species. The bark is order; the scream is chaos. The scream is frequently mistaken for a human scream.

nodes.title: Open Country -- First Truly Open-Habitat Mammal
nodes.description: The first truly open-country species in the mammalian era. Not forest (mustelids), not riparian (beaver, otter, mink), not woodland edge (skunk), not urban (raccoon). OPEN. Grasslands, meadows, pastures, clearcuts. The fox belongs to the open landscape.

nodes.title: Crepuscular/Nocturnal -- Dawn, Dusk, and Night
nodes.description: Active at dawn, dusk, and night. The third nocturnal-primary mammal in the mammalian era after the Beaver (62) and the Raccoon (70). Crepuscular peak activity -- the fox hunts the edges of the light. Dawn and dusk are fox time.

nodes.title: PNW Native Fox Debate -- Cascadensis vs. Introduced
nodes.description: Red Foxes in western Washington are a complex mix: native Cascade Red Fox (V. v. cascadensis, mountain subspecies, conservation concern) and introduced eastern Red Fox (lowland, brought for fur farming). The native mountain fox is rare and possibly threatened. The lowland fox is introduced and common. A single species, two conservation stories.

nodes.title: Tenth Carnivore -- Fourth Family, First Non-Musteloid
nodes.description: Carnivora grows to ten entries across four families: Mustelidae (6), Mephitidae (1), Procyonidae (1), Canidae (1). The FIRST non-musteloid carnivore. Canidae breaks the Musteloidea monopoly. Third consecutive omnivore in the order.

nodes.title: Canid Intelligence -- Problem-Solving, Caching, Play
nodes.description: Foxes are among the most intelligent PNW mammals. Complex caching behavior -- burying food at dozens of sites, remembering locations for months. Problem-solving ability. Play behavior persists into adulthood. The fox thinks, remembers, and plays.

nodes.title: Eleventh Consecutive Original CSV Species
nodes.description: Degrees 61-71: ELEVEN consecutive original CSV species. The streak extends beyond a decade. Every mammalian entry has been an original CSV species. No substitutions needed or considered. Eleven in a row.

nodes.title: Twelfth Mammalian Entry -- The Mammalian Era at Eleven Degrees
nodes.description: Twelve mammalian entries across eleven degrees (61-71). Ten mammalian species. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), six families (Castoridae, Mustelidae, Mephitidae, Procyonidae, Canidae). Mammalian representation rises to 16.7% (12 of 72 entries). The mammalian era is eleven degrees deep.

nodes.title: The Phil Elverum / Mount Eerie Parallel
nodes.description: Both solitary. Both operating at the edges -- Elverum at the edge of indie music, the fox at the edge of the field. Elverum's music is saturated with natural-world imagery (crows, fog, wind, mountains). The fox IS the natural world. Both watchful, both present in the landscape without dominating it. E=5 meets E=2 -- the fox is loud, the hermit is quiet.

cross_references.relationship: CARNIVORA CONTINUES. Both nocturnal carnivores. Both with distinctive defense/display. Different families (Mephitidae vs. Canidae). The skunk sprays; the fox runs. Different survival strategies for the same nocturnal world.

BOTH NOCTURNAL GENERALISTS. Both intelligent. Both omnivorous carnivores. Different families (Procyonidae vs. Canidae). The raccoon manipulates with hands; the fox outmaneuvers with speed. Both thrive near humans.

CARNIVORA EXPANSION. River vs. open country. Aquatic specialist vs. terrestrial generalist. The otter plays in water; the fox hunts across meadows. Both social, both intelligent, both Carnivora. Three families apart.

ECOSYSTEM CONNECTIONS. The fox uses beaver ponds as hunting grounds -- the wetland margins created by beaver dams concentrate rodents, waterfowl, and frogs. The beaver engineers the landscape; the fox exploits it.

BOTH NOCTURNAL PREDATORS. Canopy vs. ground. Specialist vs. generalist. The owl hunts from above in old-growth darkness; the fox hunts from below in open country twilight. Both are iconic PNW predators. Both are crepuscular/nocturnal.

Twelfth mammalian entry. Tenth mammalian species. Tenth Carnivora, first Canidae. NEW FAMILY (31st). First non-musteloid carnivore. Mammalian representation rises to 16.7% (12 of 72 entries). Two orders, six families, ten species.

Open-country habitat. The first truly open-habitat mammal in the engine. Crepuscular/nocturnal predator of rodents, rabbits, and ground-nesting birds. Native/introduced complexity. The PNW's native vs. introduced fox debate.
